content:
INP measures the interaction after the landing page, and the fix starts with field evidence rather than another Lighthouse score

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I used to read a good page-load score as proof that a site was fast. It was comforting. It was also incomplete.
A page can render quickly and still make a user wait after every click. Interaction to Next Paint, or INP, measures that part: how long the page takes to show the next visual response after a click, tap, or keypress.
The practical target is straightforward. Google’s INP guidance says a good experience is 200 milliseconds or less at the 75th percentile, measured separately for mobile and desktop. The harder part is finding which interaction is responsible and which of its three phases is slow.
A fast load does not guarantee a responsive page
Load metrics and interaction metrics answer different questions.
Largest Contentful Paint asks when the main content became visible. INP asks whether the page kept responding throughout the visit. A product page can win the first test, then lose the second when a filter, menu, editor, or add-to-cart handler blocks the main thread.
INP observes qualifying interactions over the page’s lifetime and reports a high-latency result, with outlier handling for pages that receive many interactions. It is not just the delay before JavaScript starts.
Each interaction has three broad parts:
→ Input delay: time spent waiting before the event callback can run.
→ Processing duration: time spent executing the event callbacks.
→ Presentation delay: time after the callbacks finish until the browser paints the next frame.
That breakdown fixed one of my earlier misunderstandings. I assumed a slow click meant a slow click handler. Sometimes the handler is tiny. The click waits behind unrelated work that was already occupying the main thread.
Other times the callback finishes quickly, then the browser has to recalculate styles, lay out a large DOM, and paint the result. Optimizing the handler in isolation misses the actual delay.
Start with the interaction a real user lost
Lab tools are useful for reproducing a problem. They are weak at telling you which interaction mattered across real devices, routes, data volumes, and third-party scripts.
The official guidance recommends starting with field data when possible. Real User Monitoring can attach context to an INP value: the interaction target, interaction type, route, and timings for the slow phases.
Use the web-vitals library’s attribution build or an equivalent RUM integration. Capture enough context to answer these questions:
→ Which element received the interaction?
→ Was it a click, tap, or keypress?
→ Which page and application state were active?
→ How much time went to input delay, processing, and presentation?
→ What device class and browser were involved?
Do not collect the user’s typed value or sensitive DOM content. A stable component name or carefully chosen selector is enough.
Aggregate by interaction, not only by route. “Checkout has poor INP” is too broad to fix. “Applying a coupon on mobile accounts for most slow checkout interactions” is a starting point.
Field data also protects you from optimizing an impressive trace nobody experiences. A synthetic test may hammer the navigation menu immediately after load. Your users may spend ten minutes in a data grid before the slow interaction appears.
Once you have a target, reproduce the flow in Chrome DevTools. Record a Performance trace and inspect the Interactions track, long tasks, scripting, rendering, and layout work around the event.
The trace should explain the field observation. If it does not, change the test data, CPU throttling, viewport, route state, or timing until it does.
Long tasks are usually waiting in plain sight
The browser’s main thread handles JavaScript, style calculation, layout, and much of rendering. It can do one task at a time.
A task longer than 50 milliseconds is classified as a long task. Google’s long-task optimization guide calls the time beyond that threshold the task’s blocking period.
This does not mean every 51-millisecond function automatically causes bad INP. It means the main thread had no chance to service another queued interaction during that task.
Common sources are ordinary:
→ Hydrating a large application after content appears.
→ Parsing or transforming a large response.
→ Rendering hundreds of rows after one filter change.
→ Running validation across an entire form on each keypress.
→ Initializing several third-party scripts together.
→ Doing analytics, persistence, and UI work inside one event callback.
Open the longest task around the slow interaction and inspect its call tree. Do not begin by rewriting the largest framework function visible. Follow the stack back to the application work that scheduled it.
Then ask what must happen before the next paint.
For a “Save” button, disabling the button and showing progress may be urgent. Serializing a large audit payload, refreshing unrelated panels, and updating local recommendations may not be.
That separation is often more valuable than making every function a little faster.
Yielding works when the work can be split
Breaking a long task gives the browser a chance to process pending input and render a frame.
The clearest modern primitive is scheduler.yield():
async function processItems(items) {
for (const item of items) {
processItem(item); if (shouldYield()) {
await scheduler.yield();
}
}
}The continuation runs in a new task. Unlike a basic setTimeout, scheduler.yield() gives the continuation priority over similar queued tasks, so yielding does not necessarily send the remaining work to the back of a long line.
Browser support still matters. Use feature detection and a fallback:
function yieldToMain() {
if (globalThis.scheduler?.yield) {
return scheduler.yield();
} return new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
}Do not yield after every item. Scheduling itself has cost, and tiny chunks can make total completion much slower. Batch work, check elapsed time, and yield at useful boundaries.
Also do not use yielding to hide work that should not be on the main thread. CPU-heavy parsing, image manipulation, or search indexing may belong in a Web Worker. Work that does not affect the next visual response may be deferred until after the paint.
The sequence matters:

• Apply the visible state change needed for feedback.

• Let the browser paint it.

• Continue non-visual work.

• Move sustained CPU work off the main thread when possible.
That is different from scattering setTimeout(..., 0) through a codebase and hoping the metric improves.
Processing time is only one-third of the problem
If input delay is high, the responsible code may run before the interaction. Reduce startup work, defer nonessential scripts, split large bundles by route, and avoid initializing hidden features.
Third-party JavaScript deserves its own inventory. Tag managers make it easy to add scripts without making their main-thread cost visible in code review. Record a trace with the script enabled and disabled. Attribute the result; do not guess.
If processing duration is high, keep the callback focused. Avoid repeated parsing, synchronous storage access, full-list transformations, and framework updates that touch more state than the interaction changed.
If presentation delay is high, inspect rendering. A small state update can invalidate style or layout across a large subtree. Reduce DOM size, virtualize long lists, avoid layout thrashing, and check whether reading layout values between writes forces synchronous recalculation.
This is where a generic “reduce JavaScript” recommendation becomes frustrating. The right fix depends on the slow phase.
A page with a 250-millisecond input delay does not primarily need a faster event handler. A page with a 20-millisecond callback and a 300-millisecond presentation delay does not primarily need code splitting.
Measure the phase before choosing the remedy.
Verify the percentile, not your nicest click
After the change, repeat the same lab flow under the same conditions. Confirm that the target interaction improved and that the remaining work still completes correctly.
Then watch field data. Core Web Vitals use distributions, not a single developer laptop. A fix that helps a current desktop but adds memory pressure on lower-end mobile hardware may move the problem instead of removing it.
Segment the result by device and route. Keep an eye on functional consequences:
→ Did yielding introduce stale intermediate state?
→ Can the user trigger the action again while deferred work is unfinished?
→ Does a worker message arrive after the component unmounts?
→ Did lazy initialization move a delay into the first interaction?
Performance fixes are concurrency changes surprisingly often. Treat them with the same care.
Set a regression budget around the interaction you fixed. Bundle size is useful, but it is not a direct proxy. A small script can run expensive work repeatedly; a larger lazy-loaded module may never block the critical interaction.
The useful budget is behavioral: the coupon action, menu open, or editor keystroke remains below an agreed threshold in field data.
Fix one click all the way through
Choose the highest-volume slow interaction from your RUM data.
Reproduce it. Name the slow phase. Find the long task or rendering work. Move only the necessary visual update before the next paint. Split, defer, or relocate the rest. Verify the same interaction in the field.
Then repeat.
A green load score is not dishonest. It simply answered a smaller question than I thought it did. Users do not experience metrics one at a time. They experience the page that appeared quickly and the button that ignored them afterward.
INP makes that second moment visible. The work is to trace it back to one interaction and remove the waiting without breaking the action.
For more scenario-based practice across JavaScript, React, APIs, Git, AWS, SQL, and microservices, Froquiz has 10,000+ questions plus a Senior Dev Challenge built around decisions rather than syntax.
→ Froquiz
